`pip install .` is the currently recommended method for installing from a source folder. The `setup.py install` method uses setuptools to create and install a (now deprecated) `egg` package, whereas `pip install .` will make a temporary `wheel` and install that with pip.
`pip install .` is also more future proof because build-system agnostic. In the future, one may use a different build tool than setuptools, and the next pip version will allow to specify custom build requirements.

Ignore the below, I guess we'd hit this bug otherwise:
https://github.com/coatue-oss/react2angular/issues/72

---

The react2angular docs say that if the second argument isn't specified, then it automatically uses the PropTypes to calculate the props:
https://github.com/coatue-oss/react2angular#2-expose-it-to-angular

Since we pass the third argument, we have to pass the second, but it looks like so long as it's a falsey value, the automatic PropTypes behaviour will still be used:
https://github.com/coatue-oss/react2angular/blob/d0b8c336b2a52e05839636b7651dad959be76e7a/index.tsx#L23-L25

I think it will be easier to maintain if we use this automatic behaviour rather than manually specifying. This may also require fixing some of the current PropType errors seen in the browser console and when running the tests (NB: the runtime PropType validation only happens in development mode).

A few comments on this signature:

0. Maybe rename the function, simply, `region_adjacency_graph`?
1. `labels` should go before `image`, because it is possible to create a RAG without an image, but not without a label map. Indeed, `image` can default to `None`.
2. I don't like `connectivity=2` as the default because it makes little sense for 3D images. `connectivity=1` works, as well as `connectivity=None` which would get replaced by `labels.ndim` inside the function. I have no strong preference between these two options, but I *slightly* prefer `1` because it can be read in the function signature, rather than the docstring.
3. I think the callbacks should default to `None`. A basic RAG creation function should just create a RAG, based on the definition (adjacent labels have an edge in the graph), and nothing more.
4. In addition to node descriptions, there will also, in many cases, be edge descriptions. So we need both `node_describe_func` (maybe `node_descriptor`?) and `edge_describe_func`.
5. (I don't have a solution for this right now, but something to think about.) There is a very strong coupling between the `describe_func` and `weight_func`. Indeed, it would almost certainly be a 1-1 mapping between descriptors and merge functions. Given this, it might be worthwhile thinking about how to encapsulate these two parameters into a single one.

These comments are just details, though. Overall I have to concur with @JDWarner: a very clean first PR! =)

I've divided readonly flag into 2 flags: readonly and system. Before this, readonly flag restricted dropping and creating tables (it was used in system models, and it was suitable for them). But now I've added Merge engine. Table with this engine can be created or dropped, but INSERTs are restricted. So I needed a mechanism to control these restrictions separatly: now readonly flag controls INSERTs only, and system flag controls both INSERTs and CREATE/DROP TABLE.

I'm setting ``reverse=True`` for consistency with the grammar, since strings are sorted in reverse there. We want this because the parser is greedy (if we have a rule that has multiple options for the right hand side, the first one that succeeds is the one that is used). For example, if we have `1400` in the query and both `1400` and `1` are valid numbers, then we want to try to match it with `1400` first. Otherwise, it will consume `1` and then fail to match anything with `400`.

In other words, I have not set `ETCDCTL_PEERS` explicitly on any host in the test cluster, so an empty value for `ETCDCTL_PEERS` gets passed by each system unit into its corresponding container and the modified logic in each boot script is constructing `ETCDCTL_PEERS` successfully since it hasn't been set.

I think this highlights the difficulty of working with `IGangaFile` given its dual role as both input and output files which is especially compounded by the special case of `LocalFile`.

For a standard `IGangaFile`, `put` should copy the file from the local machine to some remote place and `get` should copy the file from come remote place to the local machine. Normally a job using a `IGangaFile` as an input would read the file *from* the remote location whereas a job using it as an output would write it *to* there when done. When used as an inputfile the schema attributes need to be interpreted differently to when it's an output file: e.g. `MassStorageFile`'s `namePattern` is used to find the file on the remote file system when it's an input file but is used to find the file on the WN system when it's an outputfile.

As well as input/output differences there's also the difference between file handling done on the WN (e.g. `DiracFile`) and files sent back via the sandbox to be handled on the client (e.g. `LocalFile`). There's different codepaths and potentially different meanings to the schema attributes.

Add to this the complications of subfiles and filename globbing the whole system is very unintuitive. We need to be very clear on the API to which we expect `IGangaFile`s to conform and exactly how it should be used before we start implementing it. For example, we already have a way for the user to specify where the file should be copied to in the `LocalFile`/output situation which is `localDir`. Adding another argument to `get()` just confuses this. What should be done instead is make sure that all `IGangaFile` subclasses have a `localDir` schema attribute and make sure that is used consistently; It's always the source directory for a `put()` and the destination directory for a `get()`. Likewise, for `namePattern` (only ever a file glob within `localDir` for inputfile/`put`, within some WN dir for an outputfile and within the remote dir for a `get`).

* If time sync period is 30s, all regular MLE Advertisement will include Time IE, so no extra messages power cost, but yes, it will break backward compatibility;
* If time sync period is more than 30s, for example 90s, so only one in every three MLE Advertisement will include Time IE.

So I think there's actually discrepancy here. Both C2 and PyTorch just specify the list of padding in pixels for each dimension.

I think the reasonable support would be to have either:
1) arbitrary padding in pixels per dimension. This is the most general and matches for example TensorRT/PyTorch/C2.
2) one global setting of SAME to keep compatibility with TF.
